stories.title: Soon You'll Shop in Johor Bahru Much Faster — And That Changes Everything
stories.headline_kid: A new fast train link will make it so easy for Singaporeans to go shopping in Malaysia that families might spend S$1 billion extra there every year.
summary: When the RTS Link (Rapid Transit System) opens soon, getting from Singapore to Johor Bahru will take only 5 minutes by train instead of 30 minutes by car. This makes it super quick and cheap to cross the border. A study shows that Singaporeans will probably spend about S$1 billion more money per year in Johor Bahru — that's like 1 million Singaporeans each spending S$1,000 extra there!

The biggest winners will be groceries, pharmacies (medicine shops), and restaurants. Many things cost less in Johor Bahru than in Singapore, so families can save money on shopping. For example, a bottle of shampoo or a meal at a restaurant might be 30% cheaper across the border.

This is good news and tricky news at the same time. Singapore's shops and restaurants worry they might lose customers to Johor Bahru. But it's also great for Singaporeans' wallets, because choice and cheaper prices usually make everyone's money stretch further. People in Johor Bahru will also benefit because more shoppers from Singapore means their businesses get busier.

lenses.me: You might take day trips to Johor Bahru with your family for shopping or meals, and your parents will save money on groceries and treats.
lenses.family: Your family's shopping budget could stretch further — groceries, medicine, and dining out will be more affordable, and weekend trips to JB could become as common as going to the mall.
lenses.friends: Your classmates' families might plan regular JB shopping trips during school holidays, and some of your friends might even study or work there one day because the link makes it so convenient.
lenses.nation: Singapore will see some shops lose business to JB, which is a challenge, but the country also benefits from closer ties with Malaysia and workers having more choice about where to live and work.
lenses.world: This is how modern cities work together — removing barriers between countries makes life easier for everyone, though some local businesses need to adapt and compete better.

compo_words.meaning: something that is easy and doesn't take much effort or time
compo_words.example: The new link provides convenience for families who want to shop across the border.

compo_words.meaning: to gain something good or helpful from something
compo_words.example: Both Singapore and Johor Bahru will benefit from the faster transport link.

compo_words.meaning: not too expensive; something you can pay for without spending too much money
compo_words.example: Shopping in Johor Bahru is more affordable because prices are lower there.

compo_phrases.meaning: because of something that happened before; the effect or outcome
compo_phrases.example: As a result of the new link, families can save money on everyday shopping.

compo_phrases.meaning: looking at a different side of the situation; the opposite view
compo_phrases.example: The link helps Singaporean shoppers, but on the other hand, local shops might lose customers.

questions: If the RTS Link makes shopping in Johor Bahru so much cheaper, do you think your parents would shop there more often? Why or why not?
Some Singapore shop owners worry they will lose customers to Johor Bahru. What could they do to stay competitive and keep customers?
What do you think are the good things and bad things about making it so easy for people to cross borders between countries?
phrases: I think my family would... because...
The advantage of this is... but the disadvantage is...
In my opinion, shops could attract customers by...
This change is important because...
On one hand... on the other hand...

sources.name: Channel NewsAsia - Singapore residents set to spend S$1 billion more a year in JB after RTS Link opens

stories.title: Teachers in Thailand Caught Taking Bribes to Help Students Cheat on Big Exams
stories.headline_kid: Thousands of officials in Thailand are in serious trouble because they took money to change test answers and let students pass important exams they didn't actually pass fairly.
summary: In Thailand, about 6,000 government officials are facing suspension (temporary job loss) because of a massive cheating scandal. Investigators discovered that these officials accepted bribes — that's money paid to someone to break rules — to electronically change students' exam results. Some officials received as much as 800,000 Thai baht (about S$30,000 SGD) for this dishonest work.

The scandal began in June when investigators started looking into why so many students were mysteriously passing mandatory exams for government jobs. These are very important tests that students must pass to become government workers. Instead of studying hard like they should, some students paid officials to secretly change their test scores from 'fail' to 'pass.'

This is a huge problem because it means unqualified people got jobs helping the public. A bus driver who didn't study traffic safety, or a nurse who didn't learn about medicines, could harm people. Thailand's government is angry and embarrassed, and the 6,000 officials will likely face serious punishment including losing their jobs. This reminds us why exams matter — they make sure people actually know their job before they start working.

lenses.me: You're preparing for PSLE, which tests whether you're really ready for secondary school. If cheating were allowed, lazy students could get into better schools than hardworking friends, which would be unfair.
lenses.family: Your parents trust that exam results show your true level. If exams were fake, your certificate wouldn't mean anything, and employers wouldn't know if someone could really do the job.
lenses.friends: Your classmates study hard for exams because they know the results matter and are honest. This scandal shows what happens when people try to cheat the system — everyone gets caught and loses trust.
lenses.nation: Singapore's education system is known worldwide as fair and excellent because we don't allow cheating. Thailand's scandal shows why integrity in testing is so important for a country's reputation.
lenses.world: Many countries use exams to check if people are qualified for important jobs like doctors, pilots, and teachers. Cheating undermines public safety and trust in those professionals everywhere.

compo_words.meaning: illegally giving money or gifts to someone to make them break rules or act dishonestly
compo_words.example: The bribery scandal showed that some officials were willing to break the law for money.

compo_words.meaning: to temporarily stop someone from working or attending, usually as punishment
compo_words.example: The government decided to suspend the officials who accepted bribes.

compo_words.meaning: required; something you must do, not optional
compo_words.example: All students must pass the mandatory exam before they can become teachers.

compo_words.meaning: honesty and being truthful; doing the right thing even when no one is watching
compo_words.example: A student with integrity will not cheat on exams, even if they could get away with it.

compo_phrases.example: As a result of the scandal, thousands of officials lost their jobs.

compo_phrases.meaning: to damage someone's reputation or honor; to make someone look bad
compo_phrases.example: The bribery scandal brought shame to Thailand's government.

questions: Why do you think it's important that exam results are honest and fair? What would happen if cheating were allowed in your school?
If you knew a classmate had paid someone to change their exam marks, what do you think you should do about it?
The officials who took bribes broke the law. Do you think they should lose their jobs forever, or should they get a second chance? Explain your thinking.
phrases: I think exams are important because...
If cheating were allowed, then...
The punishment should be severe because...
In my opinion, honesty is important in school because...
This teaches us that... is wrong and unfair.

sources.name: Channel NewsAsia - Thailand mulls suspending nearly 6,000 officials over exam scandal

stories.title: An Old Hong Kong Restaurant Found a Clever Way to Win More Customers: Sports and Dumplings
stories.headline_kid: A famous dim sum restaurant in Hong Kong boosted its business by 20% by showing football matches and serving dumplings to fans at the same time.
summary: Lin Heung Lau is one of Hong Kong's oldest dim sum restaurants — it's been around for ages and serves those delicious little steamed dumplings and other treats from carts. But like many traditional restaurants in Hong Kong, business has been tough in recent years. So the restaurant owners had a smart idea: show football World Cup matches on big screens while customers eat dim sum.

It worked! Business jumped up by 20%, which means 20% more customers came to the restaurant. People loved the combination of cheering for their favourite teams while munching on hot dumplings and drinking tea. Football fans who might never have tried dim sum now discovered how tasty it is. And regular dim sum lovers got to enjoy their favourite food while watching exciting matches.

This is a great example of how old businesses can stay alive by being creative and mixing tradition with what people enjoy today. Lin Heung Lau didn't stop serving dim sum the way it's always been done — they just added something new that brings people together. Hong Kong's food culture and football culture came together, and everyone won. Many restaurants facing challenges could learn from this clever idea.

lenses.me: Next time you go to a restaurant, notice how mixing good food with fun activities (like sports on screen, or games to play) makes the experience more enjoyable and memorable.
lenses.family: Your family might want to visit a restaurant showing a big match during dinner — it turns an ordinary meal into a fun family event where you can bond over food and sports.
lenses.friends: Getting friends together to watch football while eating tasty food is way more fun than just meeting at a regular café. This combination works because it combines two things you enjoy.
lenses.nation: Singapore also has many traditional hawker stalls and restaurants. This story shows how adding modern elements (like live sports screens) can help local food businesses attract younger customers while keeping tradition alive.
lenses.world: Restaurants worldwide are learning that business isn't just about food anymore — it's about creating an experience and a gathering place. This is how old traditions survive and thrive in modern times.

compo_words.meaning: to make something increase or become better; to help something grow
compo_words.example: The restaurant boosted its business by showing exciting football matches.

compo_words.meaning: something that has been done the same way for a very long time; old and respected
compo_words.example: Dim sum is a traditional Hong Kong food that many families have enjoyed for generations.

compo_words.meaning: able to think of new ideas and do things in interesting ways
compo_words.example: The restaurant owner had a creative solution to attract more customers.

compo_phrases.meaning: to make people or things come together; to unite
compo_phrases.example: Good food and sports brought together all kinds of customers at the restaurant.

compo_phrases.meaning: to keep being good or popular compared to other businesses; to not fall behind
compo_phrases.example: Old restaurants must stay competitive by trying new ideas while keeping their traditions.

questions: If your family's favourite food court or restaurant added a new activity (like showing movies or hosting game tournaments), would you want to go there more often? Why?

The restaurant mixed something traditional (dim sum) with something modern (football matches). Can you think of other traditional things in Singapore that could be mixed with modern activities to attract younger people?
Do you think showing sports on screens in restaurants is a good business idea, or do you prefer restaurants where you just focus on eating? Explain your answer.
phrases: I think this is clever because...
If I owned a restaurant, I would add... to attract more customers.
Mixing tradition with modern things is good because...
This restaurant succeeded because it...
In my opinion, the best restaurants are those that...

sources.name: Channel NewsAsia - Football with a side of dim sum: Hong Kong teahouse scores big with World Cup fans

stories.title: Meet AI Workers: Super Smart Helpers That Are Starting to Cause Security Problems
stories.headline_kid: Asia is using AI agents (robot helpers) to write emails and code, but experts warn these smart machines could accidentally create big security holes if we're not careful.
summary: Imagine a robot employee who can draft emails, write computer code, update documents, and complete tasks all by itself. These are called AI agents, and companies across Asia are using them to work faster and get more done. Unlike regular software that follows exact instructions, AI agents can think and make choices on their own, which makes them powerful and useful. A bank might use an AI agent to sort thousands of customer emails, or a tech company might use one to write code for new apps.

But here's the problem: the same power that makes AI agents useful also creates danger. If a hacker tricks an AI agent into doing bad things, the agent might give away secret passwords, leak customer information, or put bugs into computer code that millions of people use. Because AI agents are so independent and smart, it's hard to predict exactly what they'll do in unusual situations. Security experts are worried because AI agents are spreading across Asia very fast, but companies aren't thinking carefully enough about protection.

This is a bit like giving a very smart and helpful robot the keys to your house — great for security and convenience, but dangerous if someone hacks the robot. Asia's companies need to develop better ways to watch and control AI agents, and make sure they only do what they're supposed to do. This is a challenge that engineers and companies are working on right now, to make AI safe and useful at the same time.

lenses.me: You're growing up in a world with AI. Learning about how AI can be both helpful and risky will prepare you to use it safely and smartly in your future job.
lenses.family: Your family's bank account, medical records, and personal information could be protected (or put at risk) by AI systems. It's good to know that experts are working to keep this information safe.
lenses.friends: Your generation will likely work alongside AI agents in many jobs. Understanding their strengths and weaknesses now means you'll be better prepared than adults who grew up without AI.
lenses.nation: Singapore is a tech hub in Asia, so companies here are adopting AI agents quickly. Singapore's government and tech leaders are also working on guidelines to keep AI safe — this is part of staying competitive while protecting citizens.
lenses.world: Every country with advanced technology faces this challenge. The countries that figure out how to use AI safely and responsibly will have huge advantages in business and innovation.

compo_words.meaning: a weak spot or weakness that can be attacked or damaged
compo_words.example: The cybersecurity expert found vulnerabilities in the AI system that hackers could exploit.

compo_words.meaning: able to work independently and make decisions without being told what to do
compo_words.example: An autonomous AI agent can work on tasks without constant human supervision.

compo_words.meaning: to unfairly use something or someone for advantage; also to take advantage of a weakness
compo_words.example: Hackers might try to exploit weaknesses in the AI system to steal data.

compo_phrases.example: In the same way that a smart employee could be tricked, an AI agent could be manipulated by hackers.

compo_phrases.meaning: looking at the opposite or different side; conversely
compo_phrases.example: On the flip side, the autonomy that makes AI agents useful also creates security risks.

questions: Do you think companies should use AI agents if they might have security problems, or should they wait until the systems are completely safe? Why?
Imagine you're the boss of a company. What rules would you make for your AI agents to keep customer information protected?
Many new technologies (like social media, smartphones, the internet itself) started out risky but became safer over time. Do you think AI agents will follow the same pattern?
phrases: I think using AI agents is... because...
The benefit of AI agents is..., but the risk is...
To keep AI safe, companies should...
In my opinion, the government should require...
This shows that technology is both... and...

sources.name: Channel NewsAsia - Why Asia's new AI workers are emerging as a cybersecurity challenge

stories.title: Actor Richard Low Shares His Health Challenge — And Shows Why Lifestyle Matters
stories.headline_kid: Actor Richard Low recently spent six days in hospital and revealed he has an incurable bowel condition, but he's learning to manage it through healthy lifestyle choices.
summary: Richard Low is a popular actor in Singapore who recently had to go to the hospital for six days. He shared with the public that he has an incurable colon condition — meaning his bowel (the digestive system) has a permanent problem that doctors cannot completely cure. The good news is that even though the condition cannot be cured, it can be managed, which means he can still live well and enjoy his life. Many people have incurable health conditions that they control through lifestyle changes.

Richard discovered that certain foods and habits make his condition worse. He's now reducing fried food and alcohol — which is smart because greasy and fried foods are hard on the digestive system, and alcohol irritates it. By making these changes, he can keep his symptoms under control and stay out of the hospital. He's choosing healthier foods, which gives his body the nutrition it needs to stay strong.

Richard's openness about his health is helpful because it teaches everyone (not just him) that health challenges don't mean your life is over. Instead of feeling defeated, he's taking action to manage his condition. This is an inspiring example for young people: if you ever face a health problem, remember that understanding it and making good choices about food and exercise can help you feel better. It's also a reminder that taking care of your body now — eating less fried food, exercising, drinking water — helps prevent problems later.

lenses.me: Right now at age 10, the health choices you make (eating nutritious food, moving your body, getting sleep) are building habits that protect your future. Richard's story shows why these choices matter.
lenses.family: Your parents probably remind you to eat vegetables and not spend all day on screens. Richard's experience proves they're right — prevention and healthy habits are easier than managing serious health problems later.
lenses.friends: Some of your classmates might have health conditions, and Richard's story shows that having a health challenge doesn't make you weak or different — it just means you need to care for yourself mindfully.
lenses.nation: Singapore focuses on preventive health care, teaching people to exercise and eat well. Richard's story supports this message and reminds everyone why healthy living is important for the whole nation.
lenses.world: Many people worldwide face incurable but manageable conditions. The message is universal: understanding your health and making positive choices lets you live a full, happy life despite challenges.

compo_words.meaning: a condition or disease that cannot be completely healed or cured by medicine
compo_words.example: Though Richard's colon condition is incurable, he can manage it with lifestyle changes.

compo_words.meaning: to control something and keep it in good condition; to deal with something successfully
compo_words.example: Patients can manage many health conditions by exercising and eating healthy food.

compo_words.meaning: the way you live; the daily choices you make about food, exercise, sleep, and activities
compo_words.example: A healthy lifestyle means eating well, exercising, and getting enough sleep.

compo_phrases.meaning: to do something to fix or improve a situation; to start doing something important
compo_phrases.example: Richard took action to improve his health by reducing fried food and alcohol.

compo_phrases.meaning: to show others how to behave through your own actions
compo_phrases.example: By being honest about his health, Richard sets a good example for others facing health challenges.

questions: Richard changed his diet to manage his health condition. What are some healthy changes you could make in your own lifestyle right now, without waiting until you have a health problem?
Many people feel embarrassed about health problems and hide them. Why do you think Richard decided to tell the public about his condition? Was that a good idea?
Richard's condition is incurable but manageable. Does that mean his life is bad, or can he still be happy and do the things he enjoys? Explain your thinking.
phrases: I think Richard's decision to share is good because...
Healthy choices I could make include...
An incurable condition is... because...
Prevention is important because...
This story teaches me that...

sources.name: Channel NewsAsia - Actor Richard Low reveals incurable colon condition behind recent six-day hospitalisation
